question: A two-hour 'strategy meeting' with no clear agenda, where three colleagues keep going in circles forever. What goes through your head?

answers.text: Great. Space to think out loud and spin ideas. Finally, no rigid ticking off Excel lists.
It makes me aggressive. After 15 minutes I demand a clear decision or a list of to-dos, otherwise I leave.
Pure stress inside. I keep thinking about my actual work, which is now sitting unfinished on my desk.
I try to bring the quieter colleagues into the discussion and smooth out the tense mood in the room.

question: An important project goes massively wrong. The mistake was caused by an unclear company process, not by you — but it lands on your area. Your first reaction?

answers.text: I coldly analyze the bottleneck, build a fix, and communicate crystal clearly upward who or what failed.
I spend half the night documenting without gaps that I strictly followed every rule.
I already have three new ideas for how we should tackle the next project completely differently. The past is the past.
I feel guilty because other departments are now suffering from the chaos and I immediately offer my help.

question: Your boss gives you a task that is completely repetitive, extremely detailed, and in your view absolutely inefficient. How do you deal with it?

answers.text: I get into a flow. I love it when everything is exact to the cent at the end, structured and checked off.
I die inside. After 10 minutes I lose focus, get annoyed, and start doing something else.
I secretly automate it, delegate it, or find a way to slap it together 'good enough' in a third of the time.
I do it grudgingly and thoroughly. If I don't, it falls back on my colleagues, and I don't want that.

question: A leadership position above you becomes vacant. Why don't you have the job yet?

answers.text: Because I actually don't want the constant pressure, the politics, and the conflicts up there in my life at all.
Because I sell myself brutally badly. I do all the work in the background, but others take the credit for it.
Because I don't have the 'company smell'. I'm too uncomfortable for management because I keep questioning outdated structures.
Who says I don't? I actively push for positions like that. If they block me, I switch companies.

question: It's Sunday, 8:00 p.m. You're thinking about the coming workweek. What most likely triggers a bad feeling?

answers.text: The fear that this week I still won't get to my real, deeper ideas because day-to-day business is eating me alive.
The thought of the toxic mood, the unresolved conflicts in the team, and the colleagues I have to motivate again.
The feeling of helplessness. I can see exactly what's going wrong, but I'm not allowed to make the decisions to fix it.
The worry that with that many tasks I'll lose track and make a serious mistake.

question: Your manager (who knows less about the subject than you do) gives you critical but unjustified feedback. Your inner reaction?

answers.text: I tune out. His opinion doesn't matter to me as long as he lets me get on with my stuff in peace.
I take it extremely to heart for days, doubt my abilities, and think about what I did wrong.
I defend myself immediately with hard facts, logs, or documents proving that his assessment is wrong.
I roll my eyes internally. He simply doesn't understand the bigger picture, so I don't even bother arguing.

question: Under what condition can you close your laptop in the evening with a really good feeling?

answers.text: When I cracked a complex problem today or developed a brilliant new idea.
When my to-do list is completely empty and no red notification dot is still glowing in my emails or tools.
When my team had a good day, we laughed together, and I could help someone with a problem.
When we delivered a concrete result today that brings revenue, leads, or measurable progress.

question: An innovative startup offers you your absolute dream job. The catch: 20% less base salary and a fixed one-year contract. Your honest reaction?

answers.text: No way. I have fixed costs, rent, and obligations. The risk would ruin my sleep.
I do it immediately. I need the kick of building something new; security bores me anyway.
I'm more worried that a failed startup would then sit there as a fat stain on my CV.
I would like to, but I just can't bring myself to abandon my current team now.

question: You know for a fact that you're about 15% underpaid by industry standards. Why have you just accepted it so far?

answers.text: I'm afraid it will backfire and I'll end up on the chopping block.
If they pay me more, the expectations on me will also rise massively. What if I can't deliver then?
My current job title sounds fantastic. It's almost more important to me than the money; it looks extremely good on LinkedIn.
The thought of salary negotiations or job interviews is just too exhausting for me. I've made my peace with it.

question: A sudden merger is announced. Your department is being completely restructured. What's your very first thought?

answers.text: 'Am I losing my job? How long will my savings last?'
'Damn, am I now ending up lower in the hierarchy under a completely incompetent new boss?'
'Oh no, all my perfect routines and hard-earned processes are now completely for nothing.'
'The new bosses will immediately see through that I'm actually not as good as everyone here thought.'

question: You get a fat bonus every year and make very good money, but you hate your job to the core. Why are you still there?

answers.text: The golden cage: the lifestyle is too sweet. I can't go back to a normal salary range.
The prestige: when I tell people where I work, they’re impressed. The ego needs that.
The self-doubt: deep down I think I just got lucky here. Nobody else would pay me this much.
The inertia: I complain every day, but by evening I have zero energy to actually apply somewhere else.

question: Topic self-promotion and professional visibility (e.g. on LinkedIn, in big meetings). How do you behave?

answers.text: Who am I to celebrate myself? There are a thousand people who are way better than me professionally.
I don't need digital applause. My payslip and my results are validation enough.
I'd like to post something, but then it would have to be absolutely perfect and error-free. So I don't do it at all.
Visibility is power. I actively share wins because it protects my market value and keeps competitors at a distance.

question: Imagine you turn down an obvious promotion. What would be the most honest reason?

answers.text: There was no company car and no real 'C-level' title. I'm not selling myself for just a little more money.
The sheer panic of having people management responsibility, making the wrong decisions, and failing as a leader.
I'd have to give up my familiar desk, my trusted team, and my beloved routines.
The compensation for the pain was too low. That tiny raise doesn't justify losing my notice period and security.

question: In a big group, the CEO personally praises you for a massive success. What happens inside you?

answers.text: I smile, but inside I'm sweating: next time they'll expect it again, and then I'll be exposed.
I immediately deflect and share the praise with the whole team so nobody in the office thinks I'm arrogant.
I enjoy it. I make sure everyone in the room understands that this success was primarily my doing.
I use the attention coldly to pitch my next, much bigger budget or some insane idea.

question: Artificial intelligence is automating huge parts of your industry right now. How do you react?

answers.text: Panic. I check my savings and google retraining for 'safe' manual jobs.
I'm stressed because I'm not the youngest anymore. I doubt my brain can still learn all these new tools.
I immediately appoint myself the unofficial 'AI officer' to stay relevant and protect my status.
I mostly ignore it. It's not as hot as it's being cooked up to be. My processes still run fine.

question: Your company culture is extremely toxic and wearing you down, but your contract is open-ended. What do you do?

answers.text: I stay. An open-ended contract is pure gold in today's economy. I just switch off my feelings.
I stay. If I quit now, it would look on my CV like I wasn't resilient enough.
I secretly plan my exit, but I wait for the 'absolutely perfect moment'. (Which somehow never comes).
I confront management hard about the abuses. If they fire me for it — even better.

question: On Friday afternoon, you're asked to lead a high-profile, visible weekend project at short notice.

answers.text: I say yes. If I refuse, the bosses will think I'm no longer a 'high performer' and give the project to someone else.
I say yes out of pure guilt so my colleagues don't have to work on the weekend.
I say yes, but spend 80% of the weekend panicking that this visible project will go wrong.
I refuse flat out. My free time is mine, unless you double my hourly rate for the weekend.

question: A colleague on your team consistently delivers bad work. It also affects your projects. What do you do?

answers.text: I secretly redo his work or fix it at night. The main thing is that the final product is flawless.
I talk to him and offer to help with his tasks so he doesn't get exposed.
I completely isolate my work streams from his and build systems where I no longer depend on him.
I call him out in the meeting or escalate it straight to the boss. I don't carry dead weight.

question: Your new boss is an extreme micromanager. He wants to read every email draft before you send it. Your strategy?

answers.text: I adapt and build checklists for him. If he needs control, I give him complete documentation.
I openly rebel. I ignore his instructions, deliver my results, and let him rage.
I'm massively unsettled and now check every email three times for fear of making a mistake.
I try to figure out through lots of personal conversations why he trusts so little, and I calm him down.

question: You mention a brilliant idea in the coffee kitchen. In the big meeting, a colleague suddenly sells that idea as his own. How do you react?

answers.text: I boil with rage, but I say nothing in the meeting so I don't come across as unprofessional, petty, or 'small'.
I interrupt him politely but coldly: 'Exactly, as I explained to you earlier in the kitchen... Here are the next steps.'
Let him. I already have three new, much better ideas in my head anyway. He'll be stuck soon enough.
Afterwards I ask him to talk one-on-one and tell him that, on a human level, this really disappointed me.

question: HR orders a mandatory 'team-building workshop' with a circle of chairs and feeling cards. What do you think?

answers.text: Great! Finally a chance to resolve the unspoken tensions in the team in a moderated way.
What a waste of time. I'm mentally calculating how much revenue the company is burning while we're sitting here.
I play along politely, but only say harmless, safe things so I definitely don't stand out negatively.
I use the unstructured time to mentally drift off completely and tinker with my own projects.

question: An email thread escalates ('reply all'). The tone gets harsher, the CC list grows. Your role?

answers.text: I step in as a mediator, switch to the phone, and try to calm things down offline.
I end the drama with a brutally hard, extremely fact-based email in bullet points. Discussion over.
I attach three PDFs and a memo that proves beyond doubt that my department is not to blame.
I archived the email chain after the second message and I'm already working on something else.

question: Your boss implicitly expects you to check emails on the weekend, even though it's not in your contract. Why do you do it anyway?

answers.text: Because I'm panicking that Monday morning will bring a huge mess that I won't be able to control anymore.
Because the wave of layoffs is rolling in and I need to make sure I'm seen as indispensable.
Because I want the boss to see how insanely committed I am. That secures my position in the alpha ranking.
Because if I don't, the work will land on my poor colleague. And they're already at their limit.

question: You have to onboard a new employee. What's your approach?

answers.text: I hand them a 50-page wiki I wrote myself and expect them to stick to the processes.
First I invite them for coffee, introduce them to everyone, and make sure they feel emotionally safe.
I explain the big vision, put a complex problem in their hands, and see if they can swim.
I give them a hard, measurable task right away. If they screw it up, I know where I stand.

question: If you're completely honest: what would be your biggest weakness if you had to lead a team?

answers.text: I'd avoid giving hard, negative feedback because I hate conflict.
I'd micromanage too much because I simply don't trust others to do it as perfectly as I would.
I'd probably be too impatient, harsh, and fire people too quickly if they waste my time.
I'd drown in chaos, forget administrative stuff, and overwhelm people with my constant changes of direction.

question: In the hallway, someone is being extremely toxic and gossiping about a colleague who isn't in the room. What's your stance?

answers.text: I remove myself from the situation without comment. Gossip is unprofessional and inefficient.
I speak up, say exactly what I think, and shut the gossip down immediately.
I stand there silently, maybe laugh along out of politeness, but feel miserable inside.
Later I quietly seek out the colleague affected and try to support them emotionally.

question: Your boss delegates a massive 'no-win project' (high risk, low budget) entirely to you. Why do you accept it?

answers.text: I just don't dare say 'no' to my boss's face.
I use it as leverage. If I save the thing, the glory is mine and I demand a raise.
Because I'm the only one on the team capable of getting the chaos under structural control.
I just ignore the absurd requirements and use the project as a sandbox for a completely new method.

question: Brainstorming session: someone throws a completely unrealistic, absurd idea into the room. Your reaction?

answers.text: I shut it down immediately: 'We don't have the budget for that. Takes too long. Next suggestion.'
I go all in and take the idea even further into madness. Sometimes innovation comes out of insanity.
I point out the compliance and process problems this idea would immediately create.
I'd rather stay quiet. Even if I like the idea, I don't want to embarrass myself in front of the group.

question: After a tough meeting with heated debates, you go back to your desk. What do you do first?

answers.text: I immediately write a memo with all decisions and send it out so nobody can backtrack later.
I spend hours wondering whether I phrased something too harshly at minute 14 and whether colleague X now hates me.
I go straight into implementing the to-dos. Talking is over, now it's time to deliver.
I throw out everything that was said in the meeting and think up a more elegant solution.

question: A customer (or client/patient) becomes extremely demanding, ignores agreements, and starts shouting. Your first reaction?

answers.text: I absorb their emotions, stay extremely calm, and try to soothe them like a human being, even if they're in the wrong.
I coldly and sharply point out our contractual limits. If they can't behave, they're out.
I document the incident immediately and precisely in the system so nobody can later accuse me of handling it wrong.
I ignore the standard processes and look for a creative, unorthodox loophole to get rid of them quickly.

question: It's Friday afternoon, the phone isn't ringing, and you've finished all your tasks. What do you feel?

answers.text: A vague unease. If I'm not constantly delivering, the company might realize it doesn't actually need me.
Lovely! I use the time to finally perfect my folder structures and templates for next week.
Out of pure boredom, I start designing a completely new, unapproved project or system.
I walk around the office (or the chat) and ask my colleagues if I can help them with their tasks.

question: You have to get a task done, but the official company process for it is massively outdated and takes three times as long. What do you do?

answers.text: I stick to the process exactly. Rules are rules, and if something goes wrong, at least I'm covered.
I break the rule, do it my fast way, and tell nobody. As long as the result is right.
I write a 10-page concept on how to revolutionize the process (which then dies on the vine anyway).
I don't dare deviate from the process because I'm afraid of the trouble with compliance/the boss.

question: 4:55 p.m. An 'absolute emergency email' from the boss comes in. It's not a real emergency, just bad timing. What do you do?

answers.text: I do it while boiling inside. If I refuse, I lose my status as the 'most reliable employee'.
Laptop closed. The weekend starts now. Bad planning on his side does not make an emergency on my side.
I jump on it. Finally some chaos and adrenaline instead of today's boring routine tasks.
I do it immediately. The fear of being demoted in front of everyone on Monday is too much for me.

question: Management rolls out an expensive new software that supposedly 'makes everything better'. Your first thought?

answers.text: Oh God. All my self-built Excel lists and routines are worthless now. I hate this.
I immediately try to figure out how to hack the system, misuse it, or use it for completely different things.
The software is too slow. I refuse to use it until it no longer slows down my workflow.
I volunteer to help the older colleagues who are now completely overwhelmed with the transition.

question: You made a measurable, expensive mistake. It comes up in a big meeting. Your spontaneous reaction?

answers.text: Total internal shock. My imposter syndrome kicks in at 1000%. I wish the ground would swallow me.
I bare my claws and explain, using three variables, why this was unavoidable under these circumstances.
I admit it immediately and without mercy. 'Yes, my mistake. Won't happen again. Here's the plan to fix it.'
I instantly declare the mistake a 'learning' or 'unplanned prototype' and steer the discussion toward a new idea.

question: A highly motivated, younger employee joins your team and is extremely fast. What does that trigger deep inside you?

answers.text: The naked fear that my salary is too high and they'll soon replace me with this cheaper, hungrier version.
I think it's great. I take him under my wing and try to build him up emotionally and professionally.
He annoys me a bit. He lacks structure, makes reckless mistakes, and puts our clean processes at risk.
Competition. I'll ramp up my performance to make it crystal clear who's the top dog here.

question: A project (or task) completely spirals out of control. New requests keep getting added, but the budget/time stays the same.

answers.text: I put up a hard stop sign. 'If you want feature X, we cut Y or you pay more.'
I secretly work 12 hours a day to somehow still fulfill all the requests because I don't want to disappoint anyone.
I completely lose my nerve because my clean schedule and structure are now finally ruined.
I love it. The more chaotic and bigger it gets, the more room I have to build unconventional solutions.

question: The CEO gives a fiery, extremely visionary speech about the company's future — but without any concrete plan.

answers.text: I'm extremely hyped. Finally someone here is thinking big and blowing up the old, boring limits!
I roll my eyes internally. More buzzwords, while the actual work still lands on people like me.
I immediately start building a complex Excel sheet to calculate how we're supposed to implement this insane idea at all.
I'm seriously worried about how much extra pressure and overtime this 'vision' is going to mean for my team again.

question: A headhunter messages you on LinkedIn and offers a call. Why do you (usually) decline?

answers.text: Because the devil I know is better than the devil I don't know. I hate change.
Because I'm dead certain I wouldn't survive the assessment center in the new job anyway.
Because I already project a certain 'seniority' at my current company. I'm not starting over somewhere else as a newbie.
Because my probation period there would mean six months without job protection. Too risky.

question: What drains your batteries fastest and most brutally in everyday work?

answers.text: Pointless bureaucratic hurdles and slow processes that keep me stuck like chewing gum.
When colleagues constantly dump their emotional problems, complaints, and frustration on me.
Unclear instructions that keep changing, where I can't build any clean structure.
People who are chronically incompetent but still get to make decisions.

question: You wake up in the morning with mild but noticeable symptoms of illness. What do you do?

answers.text: I work anyway (from home). I panic that important balls will get dropped and I’ll be the one to blame.
I call in sick. Health comes first. But I feel horribly guilty toward the team all day.
I grit my teeth and go in. Anyone who’s always calling in sick gets passed over for the next promotion.
I work anyway. In today’s economy, I don’t want to give the boss any reason to doubt me.

question: After an absolute horror week at work: how do you numb the frustration on the weekend most likely?

answers.text: I buy expensive things or treat myself to luxury (food, clothes, car). I need to prove to myself why I’m doing all this.
I isolate myself completely, lie on the couch, and need absolute silence to calm my nervous system down.
I take a training course, learn something new, or plan the next week so I can finally feel in control.
I throw myself into a radically different, intense hobby (sport, partying, crafting) to force my head to switch off.

question: It’s 3 a.m., you’re staring at the ceiling and can’t sleep. What thought keeps circling in your head?

answers.text: 'Did I make a critical mistake in that email yesterday? What if the boss sees it tomorrow?'
'How do I get out of this hamster wheel without completely wrecking my financial security?'
'I should have been much harder in that meeting. Tomorrow I’m going to tell that idiot exactly what I think.'
'Honestly, we should build the whole product completely differently... what if we add feature X?'

question: When do you quit a job immediately, at least in your head?

answers.text: When my years of flawless loyalty are ignored and some loud fake gets ahead of me.
When I feel like this toxic environment is slowly turning me into a worse, more cynical person.
When I have to ask permission for every tiny step and get controlled like an immature child.
When I’m given responsibility for a goal, but not the power or budget needed to actually reach it.

question: Quiet quitting: when you’re completely fed up, what does your protest look like?

answers.text: I become extremely formal. I do exactly 100% of my job description — not a minute more, not one extra task.
I secretly use office time for my own projects, side hustles, or I upskill on the company’s dime.
I become deeply cynical, make blunt sarcastic remarks, and show management my contempt.
I stop being the 'good soul.' I don’t organize birthdays anymore and I don’t comfort colleagues in the hallway.

question: You’re lying on the beach on vacation. Your phone rings, it’s the boss. What happens?

answers.text: I don’t answer, but my pulse is racing. I ruin half my beach day worrying about what might have happened.
I answer. Part of me even enjoys seeing the place go up in flames without me there.
I answer because I’m simply panicking that ignoring it will have negative consequences for my job.
I reject it cold. My phone is on 'Do Not Disturb' and I don’t think about it for another second.

question: Your doctor looks you in the face and says: 'Your stress levels are dangerously high. You need to slow down.' Your honest excuse?

answers.text: 'I know, but if I ease off now, I’ll lose my hard-earned place in the hierarchy.'
'Easier said than done. My salary pays the loans. I simply can’t afford to get sick.'
'I just need to finish this one huge project and optimize my system, then it’ll get better.'
'That’s not possible. If I drop out, my whole team falls apart. They need me right now.'

question: Instead of a desperately needed bonus, HR gives you a limp handshake and the 'Resilience & Team Spirit Award.' Your reaction?

answers.text: Pure rage. I want measurable results, money, or authority. A laminated piece of paper is an insult.
I smile politely, put it in a drawer, and know damn well it’s just a cheap tactic to keep us quiet.
Deep down, I’m still a little happy. At least someone finally noticed how hard I’m fighting.
I use the attention at the handover coldly to pitch my new project in front of everyone.

question: When you’re under extreme stress at work, what mistake do you make most likely?

answers.text: I get lost in perfectionism. I format Excel cells instead of solving the actual big problem.
I turn into a steamroller tyrant. I run over colleagues and hurt feelings to get the job done.
I say yes to everything, take on other people’s tasks, and eventually drown in a mountain of чужой work.
I start five new solutions at once, finish none of them, and throw the team into total chaos.

question: What life lie do you tell yourself most often on Sunday evening?

answers.text: 'Next week will definitely be calmer. Once I get project X off my plate, everything will relax.' (It never does).
'I’ll do this for another 2-3 years for the CV/the money, and then I’ll find something that actually fulfills me.'
'I’m just not good/fast enough yet. I need to try even harder next week.'
'Honestly, I’m indispensable. The company would go under without me, so I have to put up with this circus.'

question: Your primary work tool (whether it's software, a machine, or a system) is completely outdated and keeps crashing. What do you do?

answers.text: I build myself an unofficial workaround or cobble together my own solution to get around the problem.
I put massive pressure on management. Without proper equipment, I refuse to deliver full performance.
I document every crash meticulously so nobody can pin the delays on me in the end.
I somehow make do with it and try to help the colleagues who are even less technically able to handle it.

question: Management introduces a completely absurd performance metric that ignores the real quality of your work. Your tactic?

answers.text: I optimize my work coldly to maximize that number. If they want to measure bullshit, they get bullshit.
I spiral into stress. I want to deliver real quality, but at the same time I have to meet the targets. I burn out between two stools.
I track the number down to the last detail and stick strictly to the new rule, even if the process gets more bureaucratic because of it.
I openly mock it and, without being asked, present a completely different, better measurement concept.

question: You're dealing with an extremely difficult stakeholder (customer, client, or patient) who is blocking your expertise. How do you react?

answers.text: I cut the emotional tie. I deliver exactly what the contract or process says - not one millimeter more.
I take it very personally. I invest even more time to find a personal connection and 'crack' him.
I stick exactly to the guidelines. As long as I don't make a formal mistake, I'm safe.
I try a completely unconventional, maybe even risky approach to break the pattern.

question: Five minutes before the end of the workday, a massive, unforeseen emergency happens in your area of responsibility. What happens inside you?

answers.text: Pure adrenaline. Finally, real chaos! I hit top gear and love solving the problem creatively.
I immediately take command without being asked, delegate hard, and switch off my empathy completely.
I'm stressed, but I run through the emergency protocol exactly like a machine, the one I was trained for.
My first thought is concern for the people, customers, or colleagues who are now suffering under this crisis.

question: Another department throws a completely unrealistic, badly planned task over the fence at you.

answers.text: I reject it aggressively. 'Not our problem. Your planning is terrible. Come back when you've done your homework.'
I do it gritting my teeth to avoid an open war between departments and keep the peace.
I write a page-long email proving that this violates every agreement and SLA.
I completely ignore their stupid requirements and build the solution the way I think is more future-proof anyway.

question: At a party, someone asks what your core job as {{JOB_NAME}} actually is. What's your honest, sarcastic answer?

answers.text: 'I make sure the place doesn't collapse under the weight of its own inefficiency and incompetence.'
'I'm basically just a professional firefighter and the emotional trash can for my team.'
'I move data/processes from A to B and make sure nobody can sue us in the end.'
'I try to push innovation forward, but spend 80% of my time fighting bureaucracy.'

question: A newcomer comes straight from training/university and keeps quoting theories that don't work at all in your practice.

answers.text: I verbally shred him. 'Forget everything you learned. Out here, the wind blows differently.'
I let him (safely) run straight into the wall once on purpose so he learns the lesson himself.
I take him aside and gently translate the theory into the hard facts of our practice without demotivating him.
I put our internal process documents in his hands. Whoever sticks to those won't make mistakes.

question: Your most important resources (budget, material, or staff) are suddenly cut by 20%. What do you do?

answers.text: I warn management in writing that quality will now drop by exactly 20%. Not my problem.
I work unpaid overtime to somehow make up for the shortage because I can't let the team and clients down.
I immediately cut the 20% of tasks that bring the least value, without asking anyone for permission.
I see it as a sporting constraint. Budget cuts often create the most innovative hacks.

question: A new, disruptive trend (e.g. artificial intelligence or new laws) is sweeping through your industry. Your inner attitude?

answers.text: Fascination. I'm the first to try it, break it, and see what crazy thing we can build with it.
Cool calculation. I only use it if it measurably saves time or money. I ignore the hype.
Fear. Will my hard-earned expertise soon be devalued or replaced by this?
Concern. Won't this automation strip the last bit of humanity out of our profession?

question: If you had a magic wand for your career as {{JOB_NAME}}, what would you wish for today?

answers.text: A position with absolute decision-making power, maximum budget, and absolutely zero micromanagement from above.
A role where I get to invent the future every day, but someone else handles all the administrative crap.
A rock-solid, perfectly structured job with no surprises that I can leave at the office at 5 p.m.
A role where I have real, deep impact on people without burning out mentally in the process.

question: When you're sitting in the office and thinking about quitting: what does your secret inner fantasy look like?

answers.text: I slam my boss with the collected hard facts of his incompetence and walk out with my head held high.
I just disappear quietly, no drama. I dream that the next day all my knowledge is suddenly gone.
I start my own thing with some radical, crazy idea and prove to everyone that my way was the better one.
I have this fantasy often, but then I think about my favorite colleagues and immediately feel guilty.

question: You hand in your resignation. Your boss panics and immediately offers you 20% more pay. What happens?

answers.text: I stay. 20% more security is a massive cushion for my fixed costs. Emotions aside.
I turn it down cold. Too late. Anyone who only pays me when I leave has already lost my respect.
I stay. But only because the thought of having to prove myself all over again in a completely new company actually freaks me out.
I take the money, negotiate a new title on top, and coast on it to push my market value.

question: You're thinking about a complete career switch into a different industry. What holds you back the most?

answers.text: The fear that I'd have to start financially from the bottom again and lose my standard of living.
Losing my 'seniority'. I don't want to be the clueless beginner while 25-year-olds explain the world to me.
The missing formal certificate. Without the exact paper / certification for the new industry, I don't trust myself to do it.
I have so many different interests that I can't commit to just one new direction anyway.

question: What's the absolute horror thought for you when an interview is coming up?

answers.text: That they'll ask me technical questions I don't have a perfect answer to, and I'll be exposed as a 'fraud'.
That I have to contort myself and play along with this fake 'we're one big family' HR game.
That I get rejected. My ego can barely handle being turned down when I've already opened up.
That I have no idea what to answer when they ask: 'Where do you see yourself in 5 years?' (I hate that kind of planning).

question: If we analyze your CV without mercy: where is the biggest 'weak spot' from your point of view?

answers.text: On paper I look like a rigid bureaucrat, because my real problem-solving skills don't fit into any certificate.
I undersell myself. I use modest words even though I've effectively carried entire departments for years.
My CV is messy. I've switched jobs or quit things more often when the rigid structures had enough of me.
It doesn't really have a weak spot, it's strong. But I'm worried my salary expectations will scare off HR.

question: The company forces you into 6 months of paid sabbatical. What do you do after week 2?

answers.text: I immediately build my own project or side business, because my brain rots without creative output.
I help friends, get involved socially, or slip into the role of the 'caretaker' in my private life.
I use the time to obsessively keep learning (certificates, courses) to boost my market value when I return.
I do absolutely nothing. I optimize my private life like a project and enjoy total control over my time.

question: Topic: self-employment / freelancing. Why haven't you fully taken the leap yet?

answers.text: I'm not a salesperson. I hate having to market myself and chase clients.
The taxes, the bookkeeping, the legal risks. One bad invoice and you're standing with one foot in prison.
I need a solid team around me. The isolation as a lone fighter would drain me emotionally.
I've already tried it or I'm doing it on the side. My main worry is more that I'll get bored with it quickly.

question: We all have a tolerance limit. What small, almost ridiculous thing would push you straight into quitting?

answers.text: If they dictate my equipment to me (for example, forcing the cheapest Windows laptop on me) and wreck my workflow.
If my boss deletes my Excel sheet or my clean process and replaces it with a shaky makeshift solution.
If I'm forbidden from solving a simple 5-minute task my own way because 'the process doesn't allow it'.
If a colleague on my team gets unfairly yelled at and I'm the only one who has to watch.

question: Tomorrow you have the all-decisive talk about more pay. How do you prepare tonight?

answers.text: I write a cold-blooded list of my KPIs and wins. If they don't pay, I know my exit plan.
I practice in the mirror because I'm terrified of going soft or backing down at the crucial moment.
I collect salary tables from the internet so I can justify my demand completely and objectively.
I think more about what new freedoms, budgets, or remote days I can negotiate. Money alone isn't enough for me.

question: If you start tomorrow in a completely new company, in a completely new [Cluster]: what's your absolute priority 1?

answers.text: Real trust. I never want to have to justify every tiny decision again.
Human warmth. I need a team that doesn't constantly stab each other in the back.
Absolute clarity. I want to know exactly how I'm being measured and what the process is.
Money and autonomy. I want to be paid fairly for my results and be able to work in peace.
